text:
Admission Date:  1977-12-17     Discharge Date:  1988-04-21

Date of Birth:   1975-03-25     Sex:  M

Service:  TRANSPLANT SURGERY

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient is a 54 year old
male with a history of hepatitis C cirrhosis, history of end
stage liver disease with varices, ascites, encephalopathy,
who was transferred from Fairmont General Hospital Hospital.  The patient has
had multiple admissions to Keller Army Community Hospital, most recently was three weeks prior to this
admission, where he was treated for hypotension and
encephalopathy and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.  He was
discharged from that hospitalization to Fairmont General Hospital Hospital and
now is returning for liver transplant.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Hepatitis C.
2.  Hepatitis B.
3.  Intravenous drug use.
4.  End stage liver disease.
5.  Ascites.
6.  History of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:  Appendectomy.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:
1.  Midodrine 7.5 mg p.o. three times a day.
2.  Octreotide 100 mcg p.o. three times a day.
3.  Lactulose 30cc q2hours.
4.  Protonix 40 mg p.o. twice a day.
5.  Actigall 300 mg p.o. three times a day.
6.  Levaquin 250 mg p.o. once daily.
7.  Paxil 10 mg p.o. once daily.

ALLERGIES:  Lactose intolerance.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  On examination, the patient was
afebrile, with heart rate 94, blood pressure 114/70.  He
appeared jaundiced.  He had scleral icterus.  His heart was
regular.  His chest was clear to auscultation bilaterally.
The abdomen was soft, distended, and had a dressing on the
right abdomen which was a prior paracentesis site.  He had
bilateral edema 2+.

LABORATORY DATA:  On admission, laboratory values were
significant for hematocrit 27.3, platelet count 21,000.
Sodium 129, blood urea nitrogen 58, creatinine 1.6, AST 89,
ALT 48, alkaline phosphatase 85, bilirubin 22.3, albumin 3.7.
Prothrombin time 20.4, partial thromboplastin time 54.7, INR
2.8.

Serologies - The patient was hepatitis B surface antigen
negative, hepatitis B surface antibody borderline, hepatitis
C antibody positive, hepatitis A negative.  HIV negative.
Varicella IgG positive.  RPR negative.  R&A titer for
hepatitis C was 500,000, genotype IV and he was EBV IgG
positive and IgM negative and CMV IgG negative.

Chest x-ray showed small pleural effusions bilaterally and
minor atelectasis.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  On the night of admission, the patient was
taken to the operating room where he underwent an orthotopic
liver transplant.  Intraoperatively, the patient received
five liters of crystalloid, 13 units of fresh frozen plasma,
11 units of packed red blood cells, five units of cell Brown,
seven six packs of platelets, four units of cryoprecipitate,
and he produced 1700cc of urine.  Estimated blood loss was
two liters.  He was transferred to the Surgical Intensive
Care Unit ventilated but in stable condition.  The patient's
hemodynamics in the early postoperative period were stable.
Early postoperative day number one, the patient was weaned
from sedation and when he was fully awake and following
commands was extubated without incident.  He underwent
angiography of the hepatic artery which showed patent artery.
He had received Simulect 20 mg at the time of the surgery and
one gram of Solu-Medrol and one gram of CellCept.  He
remained stable on the early postoperative course with good
hemodynamics as monitored via the pulmonary artery catheter.
He required repletion of platelets over the early
postoperative course.  At no time was there any evidence of
active bleeding.  By postoperative day number four, the
patient was transferred to the floor for the remainder of his
recovery.  On postoperative day number six, the patient
underwent a second angiography which showed a patent artery
and at this point, his liver function tests and bilirubin had
been steadily declining in an appropriate manner.  His diet
had been advanced.  The patient had been ambulating.  The
patient continued to require intermittent platelet
transfusions and a Heparin dependent antibody test was
negative at this time.  He required less frequent
transfusions at this point and his current platelet count is
82,000 at the time of discharge.  The patient's renal
function has remained stable with a creatinine of 1.8 and a
blood urea nitrogen of 47.  He was started on diuretic
therapy on postoperative day number eleven and he has
responded appropriately.  His edema is notably less severe.

The patient's immunosuppression has been followed and
adjusted accordingly.  He will be discharged on CellCept [**Pager number **]
mg twice a day, Prednisone 15 mg once daily and Cyclosporin
100 mg twice a day.  Levels will be followed as an
outpatient.

The patient received Wilson N Jones Regional Medical Center - Behavioral Health Services Clinic consultation for
hyperglycemia.  They have helped adjust his insulin sliding
scale and Lantus insulin doses appropriately.  He will
follow-up in their clinic in one week.

The patient is stable and ready for discharge and follow-up
in the Eating Recovery Center A Behavioral Hospital Clinic.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Hepatitic C cirrhosis, status post orthotopic liver
transplant.
2.  Diabetes mellitus.
3.  Ascites.
4.  History of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
5.  Hepatitis C.
6.  Hepatitis B.
7.  Intravenous drug use.

MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE:
1.  Lasix 40 mg p.o. twice a day.
2.  Valcyte 450 mg p.o. once daily.
3.  Protonix 40 mg p.o. once daily.
4.  Bactrim single strength one p.o. once daily.
5.  Fluconazole 400 mg p.o. once daily.
6.  Oxycodone 5 mg p.o. q6hours p.r.n.
7.  Colace 100 mg p.o. twice a day (only if taking
Oxycodone).
8.  Humalog sliding scale.
9.  Lantus 10 units subcutaneous q.h.s.
10. CellCept [**Pager number **] mg p.o. twice a day.
11. Prednisone 15 mg p.o. once daily.
12. Cyclosporin 100 mg p.o. twice a day.

FOLLOW-UP:  The patient will follow-up with Eating Recovery Center A Behavioral Hospital Clinic
on Wednesday, appointment has been made.  The patient will
follow-up with Wilson N Jones Regional Medical Center - Behavioral Health Services Clinic and will call for an appointment
for next week.  He has been instructed to keep a log of his
blood sugar and insulin dosing.




                          Meredith Garner Robin Murray, M.D.  56992

Dictated By:Stevens

MEDQUIST36
